• 
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Adrienne Tissier, Treasurer 
Club 19 
P.O. Box 3113 
Daly City, CA 
94015-0113 
Dear Ms. Tissier: 
August 22, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-261 
You have asked several questions concerning the provisions 
of Proposition 73 which restrict the use of campaign funds 
commencing January 1, 1989. 
The specific statute in question 
is Government Code Section 85306, which provides: 
Any person who possesses campaign funds on the 
effective date of this chapter may expend those funds for 
any lawful purpose other than to support or oppose 
candidacy. 
On September 8, 1988, the Fair Political Practices 
commission will hold a hearing to adopt emergency regulations 
to implement section 85306. 
The hearing will commence at 
9:00 a.m., at the Commission offices, 428 J Street, Suite 800, 
Sacramento. 
We anticipate that the emergency regulations 
adopted at this hearing will determine the answers to most of 
the questions presented in your letter. 
The Commission has directed its staff to prepare draft 
regulations covering the possible alternative interpretations, 
and to recommend which alternatives the Commission should adopt 
as emergency regulations at the September 8 meeting. 
These 
draft alternative regulations and staff recommendations will be 
sent to you and other interested persons approximately one week 
before the Commission meeting. 
You are invited to attend the 
Commission meeting and you will be afforded an opportunity to 
present comments to the Commission. 
We are creating a list of persons who plan to testify at 
the September 8 Commission meeting. 
Please contact Jeanette 
Turvill at (916) 322-5901 not later than September 7 if you

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
october 2, 1989 
Adrienne Tissier, Treasurer 
Club 19 
P.O. Box 3113 
Daly City, CA 
94015-0113 
Dear Ms. Tissier: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-261 
In June 1988, you first wrote to us concerning the effect of 
Government Code section 85306, as added by Proposition 73,1/ 
on 
the use of campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989. 
This 
topic has been the subject of litigation for the last several 
months. 
We have included your name on our mailing list for 
Propositions 68 and 73, and thereby attempted to keep you informed 
of the Commission's interpretation of the law during this time 
period. 
The purpose of this letter is to inform you of the most 
recent development on this issue. 
On September 14, 1989, a federal court ruled that section 
85306 is unconstitutional and enjoined the Fair Political 
Practices Commission from enforcing that law. 
(Service Employees 
International Union v. Fair Political Practices Commission, Case 
No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of 
California.) 
A copy of the court order is enclosed. 
Accord~ngly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 
may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective 
office. 
We caution that candidates and elected officers who 
possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any 
other candidate for elective office. 
(Section 85304.) 
We also 
caution that these funds may not be used for personal 
expenditures, as defined in Elections Code Sections 12400-12407. 
Attached is a Commission advice letter which explains the 
procedures for transferring these funds to a current campaign 
account. 
1/ Proposition 73 was adopted by the voters in June 1988. 
amended the Political Reform Act (Government Code sections 
91015.) 
All references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise indicated.

94015-0113, 
Dear Ms. Tissier: 
July 21, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 88-261 
Your letter requesting advice concerning the recently 
enacted campaign finance reform initiatives (Government Code 
section 85100, et ~) was received on July 6, 1988 by the 
Fair Political Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions 
about your advice request, you may contact Kathryn Donovan, an 
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
Because of the delayed operative date of most of the 
provisions of these initiatives, and the numerous inquiries we 
are receiving relative to implementation, we are attempting to 
respond to questions regarding interpretation in an organized 
and efficient fashion. 
Due to the volume of requests and 
complexity of the issues involved, we will answer your question 
in a timely manner, but not within the usual twenty-one working 
days. 
(Government Code section 83114 (b) .) 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
You also should be aware 
that your letter and our response are public records which may 
be disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for 
disclosure.

sections.question: 1. May I use the funds in this account to finance, in part, an annual seminar I put on for seniors in my district? (The monies would be used for mailing notices of this seminar, advertising, rental of building for the seminar, rental of public address system, printing of study material, refreshments and other miscellaneous expenses related to the conference.) 2. Must the money in this account be "spent out" pursuant to Proposition 73? 3. Will this organization be subject to other provisions of Proposition 73?
sections.conclusion: Accordingly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office. We caution that candidates and elected officers who possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any other candidate for elective office. (Section 85304.) We also caution that these funds may not be used for personal expenditures, as defined in Elections Code Sections 12400-12407.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether campaign funds held by Club 19 (Jackie Speier's quarterly breakfast group) may be used to finance a senior constituent seminar, whether funds must be spent out under Proposition 73, and whether the organization is subject to other Proposition 73 provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Following a federal court ruling that Government Code section 85306 is unconstitutional, pre-1989 campaign funds may now be used to support or oppose candidacy, but cannot be contributed to other candidates or used for personal expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters spanning 1988-1989. Initial letter defers to upcoming hearing on emergency regulations. Follow-up letter provides substantive advice after federal court ruling on constitutionality. Incoming request letter and Committee organization form also included in file. High confidence on main Q/C sections from October 1989 letter; initial August 1988 letter does not provide traditional conclusion section.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May I use the funds in this account to finance, in part, an annual seminar I put on for seniors in my district? (The monies would be used for mailing notices of this seminar, advertising, rental of building for the seminar, rental of public address system, printing of study material, refreshments and other miscellaneous expenses related to the conference.) 2. Must the money in this account be "spent out" pursuant to Proposition 73? 3. Will this organization be subject to other provisions of Proposition 73?

CONCLUSION: Accordingly, campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may now be used to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office. We caution that candidates and elected officers who possess those funds may not use them to make contributions to any other candidate for elective office. (Section 85304.) We also caution that these funds may not be used for personal expenditures, as defined in Elections Code Sections 12400-12407.

embedding.summary: This file contains an initial FPPC response letter (August 22, 1988) deferring advice pending emergency regulations, followed by a substantive response letter (October 2, 1989) explaining that a federal court invalidated the Proposition 73 restriction on campaign fund usage, allowing pre-1989 funds to support candidacy subject to certain limitations.
